The Co-Creative Close (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 1 of 3
Speaker 1: Guys, welcome to Themeplate 19. This is The Co-Creative Close. And if you recall back
on the coaching the conversion process map, we had a third pulse point where you
really check in with your audience and give them a sense of ownership in that
conversation. This is the third part where it takes place. So this themeplate leverages a
few key principles that really help coach decisiveness. You know, we invite co-creation,
for example, which gives our prospect a sense of power and ownership of the outcome,
instead of dealing like something's being forced upon them, which creates resistance.
We also take the spotlight off of them for a moment and frame the offer as their chance
to help us fulfill our mission to help them. And it sounds selfish and altruistic, all at the
same time. But what we're doing is we're just reminding them that their decision
extends beyond just themselves, which helps coach decisiveness. But the big pull of this
email is in presenting and coaching binary choice. So instead of the prominent question
in their mind being, "Do I want this product or do I not want this product?" We create
this dynamic where, "Do I want this version of it or do I want that version of it?" And
what this does is it presupposes that they bought, that they purchased, that they
enrolled.
So this technique can be done using different payment options, different package
options, that feel particularly customized and engineered for that prospect's success. So
here's how it boils down on the themeplate. We're gonna open up with that deadline
urgency. Just put it front and center. Quickly mention the full transformation that
awaits. Declare your personal want for them to join. This is okay. You're allowed to say
why you want them to join, you're allowed to express your own fomo for them. And
then using the moment of highest tension, talk about why this is important to you in an
almost selfish way.
And ask them what they need to make it happen. Option one, which could counter a big
objection like time or money and package, or version number two, which would counter
a second big objection, perhaps their self doubt. And then you could put that ... You
have the option to add scarcity or limit on those bonuses, depending on what they are.
So let's take a look at an example and see how this actually plays out. So we have the
subject line, "8 Hours, What can I do? For closing, which one of these should I hook you
up with?" So some curiosity drivers there.
"Hey Jen, I'm reaching out 'cause I need a little favor here. In a few hours, The End the
Yoga Hustle Master Class will be shutting down for six months while we support
hundreds of new members in their quick ascend from overwhelmed and underpaid yoga
teacher to fully fledged Yoga Crush, earning what they're worth and creating multiple
new revenue streams that deepen their impact and support a life of greater flow and
abundance. And here's the thing, I really want you in here."
So it's an almost selfish call to action. "I created this program because it absolutely rips
me apart to see good hearted yoga teachers wrestle with burn out before caving to the
pressure from friends and family to go back to a normal job." So this is be honest. What
breaks your heart? What's your moment of highest tension, and how does your moment
of highest tension match theirs?
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 3 --

The Co-Creative Close (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 2 of 3
"The world doesn't need anymore overworked yoga teachers stretched more thin than
their favorite lulu's. And if you're at this critical make or break juncture of your career, it
would absolutely break my heart to see you miss out on your chance to finally step into
your fullest potential, and share that abundance and openness as a gift to all those you
serve."
So yes, this is all about why you do what you do, and now you're gonna need their help.
Which begs the question, "What do you need most from me to help make that
happen?" And here, we're inviting that cooperation, that sense of collaboration, and
them to express their own agency and will. So, "Bonus option 1: If the investment is a
little scary for you, or your time is so stretched thin that you'll only realistically be able
to invest one or two hours per week in learning and implementing, then I'm happy to
offer you an extended double your yoga rate guarantee." So we're taking these
objections, the price objection, we're taking the "I don't know if I have time to
implement" objections, and we're meeting that head on by simply just extending the
guarantee.
So "Instead of normal 30 day, you'll get a full two months to learn and implement. And
from this new place of clarity, power, and abundance, you can make a final decision
about whether or not this was the best investment you've ever made in your growth as
a yoga professional. Awesome. A full 60 days really helps me a ton. That will really allow
me the space and time to apply what I'm learning and give it an honest shot." Cool.
"Option number 2 for the next 20 enrollments" so we have a little bit of scarcity here.
"Or if you do have the time and are eager to implement right away, but you are still
feeling some stinging doubts about whether or not this is right for you, or if you, yes
you, will be capable of implementing and putting them into action, then I wanna offer
you a second option. Only pick one of the two please." So, we're really reinforcing the
fact that there's the binary choice and they have to choose only one.
"Here you'll get two personal one on one coaching sessions. We'll Skype it up to twenty
minutes each, the first in week two, another in week four, which are the critical spots
for building and maintaining momentum. This will make sure you stay on track, get the
full benefit of the program, and get over any personal hurdles that may be keeping you
from unleashing your fullest potential as a yoga professional. As it stands, this is the only
way to get personal access to me. And because I need to be very careful about creating
boundaries around my own time," you learn about that in Module 5, "I'm capping this at
the next 20 enrollments."
So there's some very legitimate scarcity there. Whenever you're including something
that's non leveraged like your time, that is not a false scarcity tactic. "Whatever you
decide, I just wanna ask that you make and fully own your decision. If the time isn't right
for you, be cool and at peace with that. But if you know that it's just the fear talking, I
invite you to step up and choose the extra bonus that will serve you most in the coming
weeks." And then closing with that binary choice again, this time giving them a chance
to co-create their package, their offer. It's kind of like the Build a Bear factory, except for
online training.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 3 --

The Co-Creative Close (Completed 05/08/18)
Transcript by Rev.com
Page 3 of 3
So there you go. That really encourages them to make a choice. It feels binary. It takes
the decision away from "Am I in or out?" And more from "Am I in in version A or am I in
in version B?" And they feel empowered and included in that choice. So that is it. We
have actually wrapped up all the themeplates here in Module 7. We do have additional
ones in the six figure email themeplate pack. So if you have access to that you can dive
in and find a few extras there as well. Either way, I do encourage you to put them into
action. There's a lot of themeplates here. It's designed to give you a wide variety, some
that are gonna maybe work better for you and your audience, some that maybe even
you feel more personally drawn to than others.
But either way, I do want you to get started and start writing a few before you dip into
some of the other ones you might find in Six Figure Email. So that is all I have for you
here. I'm gonna check out right now. And I will catch you soon. Take care guys.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com
